coredata.dc:title: An -OH group functionalized MOF for ratiometric Fe3+ sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Hui
Dong, Yingying
Wu, Yuhang
Ren, Wenjing
Zhao, Tao
Wang, Shunli
Gao, Junkuo
coredata.dc:description: Iron is one of the most important elements in the biochemical processes in all living system, both deficiency or excess of iron will lead to metabolism disorder diseases. However, Fe3+ is one of the most efficient fluorescence quenchers among the transition-metal ions because of its paramagnetic nature. The realization of Fe3+ ratiometric and self-calibrated fluorescent sensor is highly-challenging. We synthesized a novel luminescent -OH functionalized EuOHBDC (Eu2(OH-BDC)3, OH-BDC=2-hydroxyterephthalic acid) by hydrothermal reaction and in situ ligand synthesis, and used it as a rare ratiomatric luminescent sensor for Fe3+ ions. The -OH functional group facilitates both electron transfer and binding interaction between EuOHBDC and Fe3+, which lead to luminescent quenching of ligand-based emission while enhancement of a new peak emission, and thus enables ratiometric detection of Fe3+. The relative fluorescent intensity ratio (I 375/I 427) increased linearly with increasing Fe3+ concentration in the 10–50μM range with 1.17μM (65ppb) detection limit. The EuOHBDC also shows excellent selectivity towards different metal ions, particularly can discriminate Fe3+ and Fe2+ through different luminescent responses. This result clearly demonstrates the superiority of -OH functionalized MOF for Fe3+ detection, which can contribute to develop high performance luminescent probe for detection of metal ions in environmental and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
In situ ligand synthesis
-OH functional group
Ratiomatric luminescent sensor
Fe3+ ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: X-Ray data collection and structure determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51402261 and 51602301 ) and Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (ZSTU) under Grant no. 13012138 . J.G. acknowledges the financial support from the Zhejiang Provincial Top Key Academic Discipline of Textile Science and Engineering and Qianjiang Talents Plan of Zhejiang Province ( QJD1502019 ). S.W. acknowledges the financial support from the Science and Technology Department of Zhejiang Province Foundation (Grant no. 2017C37017 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Two coordination modes of 2-OHBDC ligand with Eu(III) ion. (b) The coordination structure of Eu(III) ion in EuOHBDC. (c, d) The exposed -OH groups pointing towards the pores in EuOHBDC along the a- and b-axis, respectively.
The excitation (black) and PL spectra (blue) of the origin EuOHBDC, monitored and excited at 427nm and 330nm, respectively.
(a) PL spectra of EuOHBDC with different amount of Fe3+ ions and (b) the intensity ratio of 375nm and 427nm emission of the EuOHBDC with different amount of Fe3+ ions (excited at 318nm).
The intensity ratio of 375nm and 427nm emission emission of the EuOHBDC with 30ppm of different metal ions (excited at 318nm, the I 375 /I 427 of origin was normalized).
(a) PL decay curves of EuOHBDC with the addition of 30ppm of different metal ions (excitation at 370nm, monitored at 420nm); (b) XPS spectra of original EuOHBDC and Fe3+-incorporated EuOHBDC.
Performance comparison between various fluorescent material sensors for Fe3+.

head.ce:title: An -OH group functionalized MOF for ratiometric Fe3+ sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials and Manufacturing Technology of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Textiles, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials and Manufacturing Technology of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Textiles, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Center for Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Center for Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron is one of the most important elements in the biochemical processes in all living system, both deficiency or excess of iron will lead to metabolism disorder diseases. However, Fe3+ is one of the most efficient fluorescence quenchers among the transition-metal ions because of its paramagnetic nature. The realization of Fe3+ ratiometric and self-calibrated fluorescent sensor is highly-challenging. We synthesized a novel luminescent -OH functionalized EuOHBDC (Eu2(OH-BDC)3, OH-BDC=2-hydroxyterephthalic acid) by hydrothermal reaction and in situ ligand synthesis, and used it as a rare ratiomatric luminescent sensor for Fe3+ ions. The -OH functional group facilitates both electron transfer and binding interaction between EuOHBDC and Fe3+, which lead to luminescent quenching of ligand-based emission while enhancement of a new peak emission, and thus enables ratiometric detection of Fe3+. The relative fluorescent intensity ratio (I 375/I 427) increased linearly with increasing Fe3+ concentration in the 10–50μM range with 1.17μM (65ppb) detection limit. The EuOHBDC also shows excellent selectivity towards different metal ions, particularly can discriminate Fe3+ and Fe2+ through different luminescent responses. This result clearly demonstrates the superiority of -OH functionalized MOF for Fe3+ detection, which can contribute to develop high performance luminescent probe for detection of metal ions in environmental and biomedical applications.

The first luminescent -OH functionalized EuOHBDC Eu2(OH-BDC)3 (OH-BDC=2-hydroxyterephthalic acid) synthesized by hydrothermal reaction and in situ ligand synthesis have been realized for highly sensitive and selective ratiomatric luminescent sensor for Fe3+ ion.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is widely distributed in nature water, vegetables, fruits and living animals, and plays a crucial role in a lot of biochemical processes [1,2]. For instance, iron ions for human beings are indispensable for organisms to form hemoglobin in red cells for the storage and transport of oxygen [3]. However, both deficiency or excess of iron will lead to metabolism disorder diseases. The World Health Organization recommends 0.3ppm as the upper concentration limit of iron in drinking water [4]. Thus, the efficient detection of trace amount of Fe3+ is highly needed.

A considerable amount of attention has been devoted to construct different detection methods to quantitatively detect Fe3+, such as chromatography, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, atomic absorption spectroscopy and fluorescent sensing techniques [5]. Among these detection methods, fluorescent techniques have shown high sensitivity, simple operation and excellent reproducibility and have been widely employed to detect Fe3+. To date, there are mainly three fluorescent detection types: fluorescent enhancement (“turn-on”) [6–8], fluorescent quenching (“turn-off”) [9–11] and ratiometric fluorescent detection [12–14]. In the fluorescent turn-on and turn-off detection, the fluorescent intensity can be affected by various variables, such as the concentration of the probe molecules, the micro-environmental conditions or differences in optical components between instruments [15]. By contrast, ratiometric fluorescent probes can overcome the limitations of intensity-based probes and provide accurate measurement through self-calibration of two emission bands [16,17]. However, Fe3+ is one of the most efficient fluorescence quenchers among the transition-metal ions because of its paramagnetic nature, and usually lead to the turn-off sensing, which are less sensitive and offer limited spatial resolution [18]. Thus the realization of Fe3+ ratiometric and self-calibrated fluorescent sensor is highly-challenging.

Metal-organic framework (MOF) materials, a new type of inorganic-organic hybrid materials, are self-assembled from organic ligands and metal ions/cluster centres through coordination bonding and have exhibited many potential applications in gas storage/separation, catalysis, fluorescent sensing and so on [19–38]. Different functional sites such as open metal sites, –NH2, -OH, ulfonate oxygen sites and pyridine sites, can be immobilized into MOF pores to enable their highly efficient detection of metal ions [39–45]. However, the research work on ratiometric MOF fluorescent sensors are still rare. Recently, some pioneering work have reported the MOF-based ratiometric self-calibrating sensors for temperature, PH, small organic molecules, Hg2+ and formaldehyde [46–53]. For instance, Du and co-workers reported dual emission dye@MOF composite for ratiometric sensing of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) [53]; Shi's group have successfully synthesized a bimetallic lanthanide MOF for self-calibrating sensing of glycol and 1,4-dioxane mixture [46]; Yan's group successfully fabricated novel MOF/carbon dots hybrid and Ag(I)-Eu(III) functionalized MOF nanocomposites for ratiometric sensing of Hg2+ and formaldehyde [16,50], respectively, which initiated the very promise of the ratiometric and self-calibrating approach for MOF luminescent sensors.

The -OH functional group is a Lewis basic site [54], which can donate its lone pair of electrons, and lead to electron transfer process with Fe3+. At the same time, it can also serve as coordination site for Fe3+ [55,56], and may generate a new enhanced emission of the formed complex. In traditional organic chemistry, the unique coordination interaction between Fe3+ and phenol has been widely used for color reaction. Cao and Jin's group have reported the construction of -OH functionalized organic chromophore molecules and -OH functionalized graphene/graphene oxides for highly efficient Fe3+ sensing [12,55]. However, the -OH functionalized MOF developed for Fe3+ sensing are still rarely explored. Moreover, considering the high efficient quenching capability of Fe3+, it is still highly challenging to construct ratiometric MOF sensors for Fe3+.

Herein, we report the first example of -OH functionalized MOF ratiometric probe to detect trace amount of Fe3+. An -OH functionalized MOF Eu2(OH-BDC)3 (OH-BDC=2-hydroxyterephthalic acid) (EuOHBDC) was synthesized via in situ ligand formation, the -OH functional group facilitate both electron transfer and binding interaction with Fe3+, which enables highly sensitive and selective ratiometric detection of Fe3+.

All the chemicals were commercially available and used without further purification. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were collected in the 2θ = 5–60° range on an X′Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ= 1.542Å) at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with a Thermal Fisher Scientific K-Alpha electron spectrometer. PL spectra were taken on a Hitachi F4600 fluorescence spectrometer. The photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage was 700V, the scan speed was 240nm/min. The excitation and the emission slit width for the sample were 5 and 2.5nm, respectively. The PL lifetime was taken on HORIBA Fluorolog. The excitation wavelength is 370nm, and the emission monitor wavelength is 420nm. The time range is 0–400ns.

2-methoxyterephthalic acid was synthesized according to previous Ref. [57]. 2,5-Dimethylanisole (0.60g, 4.40mmol) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4, 3.47g, 22.0mmol) were dissolved in 20mL of H2O. The solution was refluxed for 5h. After cooling down to room temperature, the suspension was separated by vacuum filtration. Concentrated HCl (assay 37%) was added dropwise to the filtrate until the pH reached 1. The resultant white precipitate was recovered by vacuum filtration and washed with copious amounts of water to remove the excess acid. The white powder was vacuum-dried overnight at room temperature.

The EuOHBDC was synthesized through the hydrothermal method via in situ ligand formation. Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (0.089mmol, 30mg), 2-methoxyterephthalic acid (0.051mmol, 10mg) were added into H2O 10mL, and the mixture was stirred and transferred to and sealed in a 20mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was heated at 140°C for 2 d. After slow cooling to the room temperature, pink plate-shaped crystals of Eu2(OH-BDC)3 (OH-BDC=2-hydroxyterephthalic acid) (EuOHBDC) were collected.
The metal salts analytes used for the luminescent sensing experiments are Fe(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)2, AgNO3, Ca(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2.

The dried powder of EuOHBDC (10.00mg) was added into 100mL H2O and ultrasonicated for 1h to get the aqueous suspension of EuOHBDC. The sensing experiments of the EuOHBDC was performed by adding 20μL aqueous solutions of different concentration of metal ions (Ag+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+) to 2mL EuOHBDC aqueous suspension and measured the PL spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-Ray data collection and structure determination
ce:section.ce:para: Single crystal diffraction data were collected on an Oxford Xcalibur Gemini Ultra diffractometer with an Atlas detector using graphite monochromatic Mo-Ka radiation (λ = 0.71073Å) at 293K. The structure was solved by direct methods and refined with full-matrix least-squares on F2 with the SHELXL-97 program package. The H atoms on the ligand were placed in idealized positions and refined using a riding model. The H atoms on the coordinated solvent O atoms could not be located. We employed PLATON/SQUEEZE to calculate the diffraction contribution of the solvent molecules, thereby producing a set of solvent-free diffraction intensities. CCDC 1571472.

The reaction between Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and 2-methoxyterephthalatic acid in H2O, 140°C produced a novel Eu-MOF crystalline material EuOHBDC via in situ ligand synthesis. The structure was characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction studies, and the phase purity of the bulk material was independently confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). Interestingly, the precursor ligand 2-methoxyterephthalatic acid was in situ reacted into 2-hydroxyterephthalatic acid (2-OHBDC) under hydrothermal condition. And trials were failed to obtain crystalline EuOHBDC when 2-OHBDC ligand is used directly for the synthesis under the same hydrothermal conditions.

Single crystal XRD analysis shows that EuOHBDC crystallizes in the space group P21/n with framework formula Eu2(OH-BDC)3 (OH-BDC=2-hydroxyterephthalic acid). EuOHBDC is a 3-D framework comprising Eu-O 1-D chains, which were further coordinated to 2-OH-BDC via carboxylate oxygen ( Fig. 1c). There are different types of coordination modes for 2-OHBDC ligand in EuOHBDC: two bidentate carboxylate groups bridging four metal ions, or one chlating carboxylate group and one tetradentate

carboxylate group to bind four metal ions (Fig. 1a). The Eu(III) ion is nine-coordinated by eight carboxylate oxygen atoms and one water molecules (Fig. 1b), with Eu-O bond lengths varying from 2.345 to 2.614Å. EuOHBDC has three-dimensional pores, and the pores along the b axis is about 8.0Å with exposed –OH groups pointing towards the pores, as shown in Fig. 1d. The experimental powder XRD pattern for EuOHBDC matched very well with the simulated one that are generated from single crystal structure analysis, which confirmed the phase purity of the bulk materials (Fig. S1). TGA analysis indicates that EuOHBDC show quite good thermal stability (up to 450°C, Fig. S2).

The EuOHBDC solid powder exhibits Eu(III) characteristic emission with the fluorescent peaks at 595, 620, 695nm (Fig. S3). When the EuOHBDC was dispersed in water, the Eu(III) characteristic emission was significantly suppressed, while the MOF exhibited strong ligand emission mainly at 427nm, which is blue-shifted from the fluorescence peaks of the free ligand 2-OHBDC located at 443nm (Fig. S4). Interestingly, the similar ligand 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalate shows significant emission ( Fig. 2)

changes when dissolved in different solvents (green emission at 510nm in polar solvents DMSO and DMF, while emission around 440nm in protic solvent ethanol) because of excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) [54,58], while the ESIPT was not observed in free ligand 2-OHBDC, the emission wavelength was almost the same when dissolved in different solvents (Fig. S5).

The most interesting feature is that upon the addition of Fe3+ ions, the luminescent intensity at 427nm substantially decreased and a wide new emission peak at 375nm gradually appeared. The addition of only 0.025ppm Fe3+ can efficiently quench the fluorescence at 427nm; the addition of 0.55ppm Fe3+ can decrease the I 427 to less than half of the original fluorescence intensity and generate obvious new peak at 376nm; and the addition of 3.0ppm Fe3+ lead to almost completely blue shift of the MOF fluorescence ( Fig. 3a). Such a significant blue shift not only enables the accurate measurement of the two emissions intensities, but also induces a large ratiometric value changes. In fact, when 3.0ppm of Fe3+ was introduced, the intensity ratio (I 375/I 427) significantly increased from 0.15 to 3.10, an approximately 21-fold enhancement was achieved (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. 3b, the emission ratio (I 375/I 427) was linearly proportional to the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 10–50μM.

The probe also showed high sensitivity toward Fe3+ with a detection limit of 1.17μM (65ppb) (Fig. S9), which is much lower than the 300ppb of upper iron concentration limit recommended by the World Health Organization [4]. The EuOHBDC exhibits one of the best performance on the detection limit among the previous reported MOF fluorescent sensors and is comparable to graphene/graphene oxide and organic chromophore molecule probes [12,55], which highlights the great potential of the -OH functionalized MOF for highly sensitive sensing of metal ions.

It needs to be mentioned that although numerous luminescent probes based on fluorescent dyes, quantum dots, graphene/graphene oxides and MOFs have been developed for Fe3+ detection, most of the Fe3+ sensors are intensity-based “turn-off” sensors in which the addition of Fe3+ usually lead to electron transfer and thus quenching of the luminescent intensity. Because the luminescence intensity can be heavily affected by any changes in probe concentration, excitation, or detection efficiency, such Fe3+ luminescence sensors have poor sensing accuracy. By contrast, the ratiometric fluorescent probes can eliminate most or all ambiguities by employing the ration of the two emissions at different wavelengths. Thus, the current -OH functionalized EuOHBDC ratiometric probe will have a great advantage in practical applications. The comparison of sensitivity and detection method of some fluorescent sensor materials for Fe3+ sensing is listed in Table 1.

In addition to sensitivity, sensing selectivity is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of fluorescent probes. Thus the ratiometric response of EuOHBDC toward other metal ions were also explored ( Fig. 4). The analytes such as Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+ and Fe2+ basically do not affect the emission ratio I 375/I 427, while only the addition of Fe3+ ions lead to prominent response. Interestingly, the fluorescence of EuOHBDC exhibits different response to Fe2+ and Fe3+. The addition of 30ppm Fe2+ did not lead to emission peak shift or quenching effect (Fig. S18), and the emission ratio I 375/I 427 remained almost the same. As the Fe3+/Fe2+ states are one of the most important redox pairs in biological systems, the imbalance of different valent iron ions in human body induces the occurrence of many diseases. The fluorescence of EuOHBDC exhibits high selectivity to Fe3+ and other metal ions, and the different responses toward Fe3+/Fe2+ also highlights its potential application in biological sensing of different valent metal ions and disease diagnosis.

According to the previous reported Fe3+ sensing mechanism in -OH functionalized graphene/graphene oxide and organic chromophore molecule probes [12,55], we speculate that there is coordination and electron transfer between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl group in EuOHBDC. Firstly, there is a new peak (marked with asterisks) come out in the powder XRD patterns of EuOHBDC after immersion in a large amount (10−2 M) of Fe3+ for two hours (Fig. S1), indicating that a new kind of structure may form after a long time of immersion, which may due to the exchange of Fe3+ and Eu3+ in EuOHBDC. However, the main framework of EuOHBDC remains the same. Then, PL lifetime and O 1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were carried out to further confirm the mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the luminescence lifetime of origin EuOHBC is 53.2ns, and it remains almost the same after the addition of Co2+ and Ni2+ ions, while the addition of Fe3+ significantly decreased the luminescence lifetime of EuOHBC to 24.2ns, which indicated that there was electron transfer between the Fe3+ and EuOHBDC. As the outer electronic structure of Fe3+ is 3d5 and there are five halffilled d orbits, Fe3+ has strong electron capture capability and the electrons at the conductive band of EuOHBDC will be easily transferred to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe3+ ions, leading to the significant decrease of fluorescence lifetime in EuOHBDC. O1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were further carried out on pure EuOHBDC and Fe3+-incorporated EuOHBDC. Deconvolution of the XPS O1s peak (Fig. 5b) indicated the presence of carboxylic and free hydroxyl functional group at 533.1eV and 531.7eV, respectively. The O 1s peak from free hydroxyl oxygen atoms at 531.7eV in pure EuOHBDC was shifted to 532.2eV on the addition of Fe3+, indicating the binding of hydroxyl oxygen atoms to Fe3+ in Fe3+- incorporated EuOHBDC. Such binding of the hydroxyl oxygen atoms to Fe3+ not only facilitate electron transfer between the EuOHBDC and Fe3+, quenching the luminescence on the emission peak at 427nm, but also induces a new emission peak, which is speculated to be a new formed Fe-EuOHBDC complex. In addition, the luminescent emission of the free ligand OHBDC only showed weak quenching effect upon the addition of 30ppm Fe3+ (Fig. S19), which indicated the new emission peak can be attributed to the Fe3+-EuOHBDC binding. And the luminescence of the solid EuOHBDC powder also showed the new enhanced emission around 380nm after immersed in 10−2 M Fe3+ solution, which further confirmed the unique Fe3+-EuOHBDC binding (Fig. S20). Based on the above experiments, we speculate that the ratiometric PL change within a short period mainly results from the electron transfer and the binding between Fe3+ and the -OH functional group of the MOF.

In summary, for the first time, we report a novel luminescent -OH functionalized EuOHBDC Eu2(OH-BDC)3 (OH-BDC=2-hydroxyterephthalic acid) for the ratiomatric luminescent sensor for Fe3+ ions. The -OH functionalized EuOHBDC was generated by in situ ligand synthesis, in which the OH-BDC was formed by in situ reaction of OCH3-BDC under hydrothermal condition. The excellent luminescent sensing ability of EuOHBDC mainly comes from the free -OH functional group pointing towards the pores, which facilitates both electron transfer and binding interaction between EuOHBDC and Fe3+. The EuOHBDC fluorescent probe shows highly sensitive and selective sensing of Fe3+ with a wide linear range 10–50μM and a low detection limit of 1.17μM (65ppb). This result clearly demonstrates the strong capability of -OH functionalized MOF to Fe3+ detection, which can contribute to develop high performance luminescent probe for detection of metal ions in environmental and biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51402261 and 51602301) and Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (ZSTU) under Grant no. 13012138. J.G. acknowledges the financial support from the Zhejiang Provincial Top Key Academic Discipline of Textile Science and Engineering and Qianjiang Talents Plan of Zhejiang Province (QJD1502019). S.W. acknowledges the financial support from the Science and Technology Department of Zhejiang Province Foundation (Grant no. 2017C37017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jssc.2017.11.013
